Build your weekly kilometers safely—maximum +10% per week—fast and free. Our calculators are updated for 2026 and reviewed regularly to keep you current.

Validate training plans before implementing them
Teaching and learning running progression concepts
explanation:
**Running training** combines science (physiology, biomechanics) and practice. Intensity zones, progressive loading, rest, and nutrition form the complete system.

## How it's calculated

Weekly mileage progression—maximum +10% per week—is a science-backed approach to building fitness safely. This calculator applies the formula to your input values, checking typical ranges and alerting you to risky increases.

## Final notes

This calculation is a reference guide. For critical decisions (medical, injury-related, or specific to your situation), consult a sports medicine professional or running coach. Values are current for 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.q: What is the 10% rule for running?
faq.a: The 10% rule recommends increasing your weekly running mileage by a maximum of 10% each week. This allows your body to adapt safely—muscles, tendons, and bones need time to strengthen.

faq.q: How do I calculate my next week's mileage?
faq.a: Multiply your current weekly kilometers by 1.10. For example: 30 km × 1.10 = 33 km next week.

faq.q: Why shouldn't I increase mileage faster than 10% per week?
faq.a: Rapid increases stress your musculoskeletal system faster than it can adapt, dramatically raising injury risk. Running injuries often come from doing too much too soon.

faq.q: Do I need a deload week?
faq.a: Yes. Every 3–4 weeks of progression, take a deload week where you reduce mileage by 20%. This lets your body recover and consolidate fitness gains.

faq.q: Does the 10% rule apply to all runners?
faq.a: It's a safe starting point for most runners. Some experienced runners can handle 15% increases; others (especially beginners or returning from injury) may need a stricter 5% rule.

faq.q: What if I miss a week of training?
faq.a: Don't try to 'catch up' with a large jump. Return to your most recent completed mileage and apply the 10% rule from there, rebuilding gradually.
Yes. Cumulative elevation gain also scales with the +10% rule. A hilly 30 km week isn't the same as a flat 30 km week—factor in terrain difficulty.
Yes, all our calculators are completely free and require no signup or login.

Results are guidelines based on the standard 10% formula, validated internationally. For personalized advice—especially if you have injuries or specific goals—consult a running coach or sports medicine professional.
